howItWorks.title: How to Add Lyrics to Video Automatically

steps.title: Upload your song audio
steps.description: Start with an MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A audio file up to 100MB. Direct upload of finished footage for lyric overlay is planned, but the stable workflow today starts from audio.

steps.title: AI transcribes and syncs
steps.description: AI transcribes the audio automatically and syncs every line in 50+ languages. Click any line to fix a word before rendering. The text follows the audio, so each line lands on screen right as it's sung or spoken.

steps.title: Preview and export
steps.description: Watch the full video with lyrics before publishing. Move the lyric block away from faces, products, or action in the frame, then export in 16:9 for YouTube, 9:16 for TikTok, Reels, and Shorts, or 1:1 for feed posts. Free to export, no subscription required.

whyChoose.title: Why Add Lyrics to Video on LyricVideoMaker
whyChoose.description: Building a lyric video by hand means typing every line, dragging text across a timeline, and checking every cue. LyricVideoMaker starts from your song audio, creates beat-synced lyrics, and renders a new lyric video without that manual timeline work.

highlight.title: Designed for videos that already exist
highlight.description: Use a music video, performance recording, wedding clip, travel montage, fan edit, or promo cut. LyricVideoMaker adds the lyric layer without flattening the video into a generic caption template.

cards.title: Your footage stays the focus
cards.description: Place the lyric overlay where it supports the scene instead of covering faces, dancers, products, or the moment viewers came to see.

cards.title: Synced text, not text pinned to the bottom
cards.description: Instead of pinning plain text to the bottom of the frame, LyricVideoMaker places synced lyrics — or captions — as a visual layer that moves with the audio and stays readable over your footage.

cards.title: Fix one line without rebuilding the edit
cards.description: If the transcript needs a correction, click the lyric line, adjust the words, and keep the timing workflow moving without starting the video over.

contentSections.heading: What does it mean to add lyrics to video?
contentSections.intro: To add lyrics to video with LyricVideoMaker today means creating a new lyric-forward MP4 from your song audio, with direct upload-and-overlay for existing footage treated as a planned workflow. The stable path starts from audio, transcribes and syncs lyrics, generates scenes, lets you edit lines, and exports a finished video.
bullets: Input today is audio upload only: MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A up to 100MB.
Directly uploading finished footage and placing lyrics over it is a planned overlay workflow, not the core stable path.
You can still create a publishable MP4 with synced lyrics, AI scenes, preview, and editable lines.
The distinction from subtitle tools is that the song drives lyric timing and visual design.

contentSections.heading: add lyrics to video specs at a glance

rows: Accepted input
Audio upload only: MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A, up to 100MB per file.
Lyrics
AI transcribes vocals automatically in 50+ languages, then lets you edit every line before export.
Sync
Beat-aware line timing keeps lyrics readable and musical instead of simply dropping captions on a fixed grid.
Visuals
Storyboard planning and AI scene-image generation create backgrounds around the mood of the song.
Exports
MP4 with original audio in 16:9, 9:16, or 1:1 for YouTube, Shorts, TikTok, Reels, and feeds.
Start
Free credits are available at the start, so you can preview the workflow before spending more time on a release.

contentSections.heading: Current lyric-video flow vs planned overlay flow
contentSections.intro: People searching this phrase often already have footage. The honest split is simple: the current product reliably creates a new lyric video from audio, while direct lyric overlay on an existing video needs more video-specific controls.

rows: Stable now
Upload audio, transcribe lyrics, sync lines, generate scenes, preview, and export MP4.
Planned overlay
Upload finished footage, extract or pair audio, then place lyrics over the existing video.
Needed for overlay
Face-safe positioning, original-video quality preservation, and drag placement.
Already useful
Create a standalone lyric video for the same song and use it beside the existing footage.
Best review step
Check whether lyrics stay readable before worrying about exact placement over footage.
Best expectation
Use the audio-first result today; treat direct video overlay as upcoming rather than guaranteed.

contentSections.heading: Best practices when you want lyrics on video
contentSections.intro: Use these checks before you render, especially when the same project needs more than one platform format.
bullets: Decide whether you need a new lyric video or lyrics over existing footage; they are different workflows.
If using the current tool, upload the clean song audio rather than a compressed screen recording.
Keep lyric lines short enough that they would still work over future footage.
Choose styles that leave room around faces or key objects if you plan to reuse the design later.
Correct the transcript before exporting, because every visual version depends on the same text.
Use vertical-safe placement if the video may become a short-form post.
For existing footage, plan where lyrics should not go before the overlay feature arrives.

contentSections.heading: Why overlay expectations need to stay clear
body: For add lyrics to video, the important choices happen before rendering. The table below turns audio extraction, face-safe placement, and future upload-video workflow into practical decisions you can make while the AI handles transcription, timing, and scene generation.

add lyrics to video works best when it respects the order of the music. Lyrics are not decoration added at the end; they are the structure the viewer follows. By transcribing first, aligning second, and designing third, the workflow keeps the words, the beat, and the generated imagery connected instead of letting a template make every song feel the same.

The practical benefit is control without timeline labor. You still decide whether the style feels right, whether a line break should change, and whether the video needs a vertical, square, or widescreen export. The repetitive parts are handled by AI, but the final judgment stays with the person releasing the song.

For direct lyric overlays on existing footage, expectations should stay clear: that workflow is planned, and it needs video upload, face-safe placement, and quality-preserving rendering. The reliable path today is to generate a new lyric video from the audio, then use the exported MP4 in your release stack.

Add Lyrics to Video takes a video you already have and lays synced words on top of it. Upload your footage, and the AI reads the audio to transcribe the lyrics — or the spoken captions — then times each line to match what is being sung or said, so the text appears exactly when it should.

It works whether the words are a song's lyrics or plain captions, because the timing comes from the audio itself rather than a manual timeline. You get a video with synced text baked in, ready to publish, without typing or dragging anything.

A good add lyrics to video also protects the listener's attention. The viewer should never have to choose between reading the line and watching the scene; the scene exists to make the lyric feel more alive. That is why contrast, line length, timing, and motion restraint matter as much as the first generated image.

The review habit is simple: watch once for words, once for rhythm, and once for platform fit. The first pass catches transcription mistakes, the second catches lines that arrive early or late, and the third catches text that works on desktop but fails on a phone or inside a social feed.

For creators with existing footage, the real win is not only speed. It is having one source project that can become a full video, a short vertical clip, and a square feed asset without retyping lyrics or rebuilding timing. Consistency across those exports makes a release feel planned.

The safest publishing pattern is to make the lyric video while the song is still fresh in your ear. You will hear awkward line breaks faster, recognize misheard words sooner, and make better choices about where the visual energy should rise or stay quiet.

The useful boundary is that AI should remove repetitive labor, not remove taste. Let it transcribe, sync, and propose scenes; then use the preview to decide whether the song needs more space, more contrast, a calmer verse, or a stronger hook treatment.

Starting with a clean song file keeps the workflow focused on vocals, beat, and structure before any visual decoration appears. Once those foundations are correct, the design choices become much easier to judge, and every exported format inherits the same lyrical backbone.

contentSections.heading: add lyrics to video checklist before export
contentSections.intro: A quick pre-export review catches most quality problems while they are still cheap to fix.

rows: Audio readiness
Accepted formats are MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, and M4A; keep the file under 100MB.
Language review
The system auto-detects 50+ languages, but bilingual hooks and names deserve a manual pass.
Timing review
Beat sync gives a strong baseline; nudge any line that lands early or late against the vocal.
Scene review
Generated images should match the mood without making text hard to read.
Export review
Check 16:9, 9:16, and 1:1 separately because readable text size changes by format.

items.question: Is this add lyrics to video tool free?
items.answer: Yes. Sign up, get free credits, create a lyric video, preview it, and export — no subscription required, no credit card needed.

items.question: How do I put lyrics on video without typing them?
items.answer: Upload your video or audio file. AI transcribes lyrics from the audio automatically, syncs the lines to the beat, and lets you edit any word before export.

items.question: Will adding lyrics reduce my video quality?
items.answer: No. Your video is processed as a finished visual layer with synced lyrics added on top, and you can export in the format that fits YouTube, TikTok, Reels, Shorts, or feed posts.

items.question: Do I need a separate audio file if my video already has music?
items.answer: No. If the music is already in the video, LyricVideoMaker can analyze that audio directly. A separate audio file is optional when you want to use a cleaner track.

items.question: Can I move lyrics so they do not cover faces?
items.answer: Yes. Preview the video and place the lyric block where it stays readable without hiding performers, products, captions, or important action.

items.question: Can I use exported lyric videos commercially?
items.answer: Yes. The videos you add lyrics to are yours to publish — channels, campaigns, and client work included. Just confirm you hold the rights to the footage and the music.

items.question: How to put lyrics on video automatically?
items.answer: Upload your video or audio and AI adds the synced lyric layer for you — no manual timing. You can add lyrics to video automatically online free with free credits, then export in the format you need.
